title: How the human body changed: apes to us

intro.focus: Showing interesting evolutionary CHANGE across the human line and its living relatives, not a proven chain of direct ancestors.
intro.honesty: Images are the best current reconstructions, not photographs of the past. Relationships are shown as supported, candidate, or context; living apes are cousins, not ancestors.

taxa.summary: A well-known early ape, before any living ape lineage had taken on its specialised body. This is roughly where the ape story starts.
taxa.physicalChange: A tailless ape that still moved as an above-branch quadruped — none of the suspensory, knuckle-walking or upright specialisations of living apes yet.
taxa.behavioralChange: Lived in warm Miocene forests, moving carefully along branches and eating mostly fruit. Behaviour was ape-general, not yet chimp-like or human-like.

pressures.change: Loss of the tail and a more upright trunk
pressures.pressure: Cautious climbing and feeding in the canopy favoured balance over a tail.

taxa.certainty: Solid as an early ape; not a proven direct ancestor of living apes.

image.creator: Paleoart, Wikimedia Commons
image.caveat: A life restoration of a closely related early Miocene ape (Proconsul) — Ekembo was formerly placed in Proconsul. Representative, not exact.

fossilSites.note: Skulls, teeth and partial skeletons of an early ape.

taxa.commonName: a late Miocene African great ape
taxa.summary: A large African ape living close to the time the gorilla and human–chimp lines were separating. Known mainly from a jaw and teeth.
taxa.physicalChange: A big-bodied great ape with thick-enamelled teeth — evidence that African great apes were diversifying near the gorilla and chimp splits.
observedTraits: known from jaw and teeth only
taxa.behavioralChange: Probably ate tough, fibrous plant foods, fitting a forest-to-woodland African setting around the time our lineage's cousins branched off.

pressures.pressure: Tougher, more fibrous foods in seasonal African forests.

taxa.certainty: Useful context near the African-ape splits; too incomplete to be a named ancestor.

image.creator: Paleoart, Wikimedia Commons
image.caveat: Nakalipithecus is known only from a jaw; a representative Miocene great-ape reconstruction is shown.

taxa.summary: Gorillas are not our ancestors — they are cousins. Their line branched off the shared African-ape stem around 9 million years ago.
taxa.physicalChange: The gorilla line went large and ground-dwelling: huge body, knuckle-walking arms, and a big chewing apparatus for leaves and stems.
observedTraits: large gut and jaws for leaves
taxa.behavioralChange: Settled into eating bulky low-quality vegetation in African forests, in stable family groups led by a dominant male.

pressures.change: Large body and big chewing/gut system
pressures.pressure: A diet of abundant but low-energy leaves and stems rewards size and fermentation capacity.
Moving a heavy body efficiently across the forest floor.

taxa.certainty: A living relative shown to make the branching real — not on the human line.

image.creator: Wikimedia Commons
image.caveat: A photograph of a living western gorilla.

taxa.summary: Our closest living relatives. Their line and ours split from a shared ancestor roughly 7 million years ago — neither is a 'primitive human'.
taxa.physicalChange: The chimp line kept a flexible climbing-and-knuckle-walking body and forest-fruit diet, while staying smaller-brained than later humans.
taxa.behavioralChange: Lives in dynamic social groups, uses simple tools, hunts occasionally — a window onto behaviours the shared ancestor may have had.

pressures.pressure: Forest life that mixes fruit trees with travel across the ground.

taxa.certainty: A living relative, not an ancestor; our best living comparison point.

image.creator: Giles Laurent
image.caveat: A photograph of a living chimpanzee.

taxa.summary: One of the oldest fossils proposed to be on the human side of the split, found far west of the usual East African sites.
taxa.physicalChange: Small ape-sized brain, but with reduced canines and a skull base hinting the head sat atop an upright spine — the first whispers of bipedality.
taxa.behavioralChange: Lived in a mosaic of lake-edge forest and grassland, plausibly moving both upright on the ground and through trees.

pressures.change: Head balanced over the spine (hint of upright walking)
pressures.pressure: Standing and moving upright in patchy woodland near water.
Shifts in diet and in male-to-male social signalling.

taxa.certainty: Debated — possibly the earliest hominin, possibly an ape near the split.

image.creator: John Gurche / Smithsonian Human Origins
image.caveat: Artist’s facial reconstruction; the skull is distorted and soft tissue is interpretive.

fossilSites.note: Distorted but fairly complete cranium, plus a femur.

taxa.summary: A fragmentary late-Miocene form whose thigh bone is one of the earliest hints of upright walking.
taxa.physicalChange: A femur built for at least some upright, weight-bearing walking — yet the arms still say 'good climber'.
taxa.behavioralChange: Mixed woodland life rewarding both ground walking and tree climbing — locomotor flexibility rather than commitment.

pressures.change: Thigh bone adapted to upright loading
pressures.pressure: Walking between scattered food patches on the ground.

taxa.certainty: Bipedal signal is real; whether it sits on the human line is uncertain.

image.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins
image.caveat: Orrorin is too fragmentary for a species-specific face; a representative early-hominin reconstruction is shown.

fossilSites.note: Partial thigh bone showing bipedal loading.

taxa.summary: Sparse teeth and bones near the human–chimp divergence, showing early dental changes.
taxa.physicalChange: Canines starting to lose their ape-like honing edge, with a toe bone read as compatible with upright walking.
taxa.behavioralChange: Woodland forager at the very base of the human line; too little is preserved to say much about behaviour.

pressures.pressure: Dietary and social shifts away from ape-style canine display.

taxa.certainty: Real but sparse; relationship to later Ardipithecus is plausible, not proven.

image.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins
image.caveat: A representative Ardipithecus reconstruction; A. kadabba is known from sparse material.

taxa.summary: A remarkably complete early hominin showing that walking upright arrived piecemeal — not as a finished package.
taxa.physicalChange: A pelvis that allowed upright walking, but kept a grasping, divergent big toe for climbing — a true mosaic, unlike any living ape.
taxa.behavioralChange: Lived in woodland (not open savanna), moving upright on the ground and still climbing carefully in trees.

pressures.change: Upright walking added without losing climbing
pressures.pressure: Woodland life that rewarded both ground travel and tree feeding.

taxa.certainty: Strong as a species; an early hominin, but not a proven direct ancestor.

image.creator: Cícero Moraes
image.caveat: A 3D full-body model of Ardipithecus ramidus (“Ardi”); posture is fossil-based, the surface is a plain render.

fossilSites.note: Partial skull, pelvis, hands and feet.

taxa.summary: The oldest widely accepted Australopithecus — the genus that walked the African woodlands for over a million years.
taxa.physicalChange: Leg bones show more committed upright walking, while the jaws and teeth stay large and primitive.
taxa.behavioralChange: Foraged in woodland and gallery forest near water, processing tougher plant foods with big teeth.

pressures.pressure: Travel between food patches in mixed woodland.
Harder, grittier foods as habitats opened up.

taxa.certainty: Strong species; close to the ancestry of later australopiths.

image.creator: Paleoart, Wikimedia Commons
image.caveat: A representative australopith facial reconstruction; the near-complete MRD cranium informs the real species.

taxa.summary: The famous 'Lucy' species — clearly bipedal on the ground, yet still built to climb, and still small-brained.
taxa.physicalChange: A fully bipedal pelvis, knee and heel-strike (and footprints to prove it), combined with long arms and curved fingers for climbing.
taxa.behavioralChange: Walked across open ground between trees, slept and fed in the canopy, and left the 3.6-million-year-old Laetoli footprint trail.

pressures.change: Walking established before the brain grew
pressures.pressure: Efficient ground travel mattered earlier than big brains did.

taxa.certainty: Very well sampled; close to the source of later lineages.

image.creator: California Academy of Sciences (model)
image.caveat: A fleshed museum model (the ‘Selam’ individual); body proportions are fossil-based, soft tissue interpretive.

taxa.summary: The first early human relative ever found in Africa (the 1924 Taung Child), showing the lineage had spread south.
taxa.physicalChange: A rounder skull and slightly larger brain than Lucy's species, with a less projecting face — but still an australopith body.
taxa.behavioralChange: Lived in the South African cave country, eating a flexible mix of plants and possibly some animal foods.

pressures.pressure: A broad, flexible diet in a more varied southern landscape.

taxa.certainty: Solid species; its exact place near the origin of Homo is debated.

image.creator: John Gurche / Smithsonian Human Origins
image.caveat: Artist’s facial reconstruction; soft tissue is interpretive.

fossilSites.note: The 1924 discovery that started African palaeoanthropology.

taxa.summary: A spectacular side-branch that solved survival with giant teeth and chewing muscles, while early Homo nearby went a different way.
taxa.physicalChange: An extreme chewing machine: huge flat molars, thick enamel, flaring cheekbones and a bony crest on top of the skull for massive jaw muscles.
taxa.behavioralChange: Processed large amounts of tough, low-quality plant food — a specialist strategy that ran alongside, and eventually lost to, tool-using Homo.

pressures.pressure: Relying on abundant but tough, hard-to-chew vegetation, especially in lean seasons.

taxa.certainty: A well-understood collateral branch — not a human ancestor.

image.creator: Smithsonian NMNH (photo: Ryan Schwark)
image.caveat: Chewing anatomy is fossil-based; hair, skin and eyes are interpretive.

taxa.summary: An early member of our own genus, with a bigger brain and smaller face than australopiths — but still a partly ape-like body.
taxa.physicalChange: Brain expanding (~510–700 cc) and face/teeth shrinking, even while arms and hands kept climbing-related features.
taxa.behavioralChange: Lived alongside stone tools and cut-marked bones, gaining access to meat and marrow — a flexible, tool-assisted forager.

pressures.pressure: Tools doing the work teeth used to: cutting and accessing higher-energy foods.

taxa.certainty: Real early Homo; its exact link to later Homo is debated.

image.creator: Cícero Moraes
image.caveat: A forensic reconstruction from incomplete fossils; soft tissue is interpretive.

fossilSites.note: Type specimen: jaw, teeth and hand bones.

taxa.role: Modern body, first to leave Africa
taxa.summary: The turning point: the first hominin with a tall, modern-looking body that ranged across continents.
taxa.physicalChange: Long legs, shorter arms, a narrow trunk and a bigger body and brain — built for long-distance walking and running on the ground.
taxa.behavioralChange: Walked huge daily ranges, made Acheulean handaxes, ate a broader, energy-richer diet, and spread from Africa to Java.

pressures.pressure: Bigger foraging ranges and heat management in open country.
Richer, more reliable diet (including meat) able to fuel the cost.

taxa.certainty: Strong as a broad grade; its many regional forms are debated.

image.creator: Public-domain illustration
image.caveat: A classic full-body illustration of Homo erectus; body is fossil-based, surface interpretive.

taxa.summary: A shock discovery: a small-brained human relative that survived surprisingly recently, found deep inside a cave system.
taxa.physicalChange: A mosaic body — human-like feet and legs for walking, but curved fingers, a primitive shoulder and a brain only a third of ours.
taxa.behavioralChange: Walked the ground and climbed; its remains, deep in the Rising Star cave, hint at deliberate body disposal — still debated.

pressures.change: Small brain kept late in time
pressures.pressure: Big brains are not inevitable — this lineage stayed small-brained and survived anyway.

taxa.certainty: Securely a species; its origins and behaviour are actively debated.

image.creator: Cícero Moraes
image.caveat: A reconstruction from well-preserved skulls; soft tissue is interpretive.

fossilSites.note: Hundreds of bones deep inside the cave.

taxa.summary: A roughly one-metre-tall human relative that survived on an Indonesian island into surprisingly recent times.
taxa.physicalChange: Dramatically shrunk: barely a metre tall with a tiny grapefruit-sized brain, yet still walked upright and made tools.
taxa.behavioralChange: Lived isolated on Flores, hunting dwarf elephants and giant rodents — a textbook case of island dwarfism.

pressures.pressure: Limited food on a small island favours smaller bodies (island dwarfism).

taxa.certainty: A real species; likely a dwarfed descendant of an earlier Homo such as erectus.

image.creator: Cícero Moraes
image.caveat: Forensic facial approximation of the ‘Hobbit’; soft tissue is interpretive.

fossilSites.note: Partial skeleton of a ~1 m adult.

taxa.summary: The broad, big-brained populations that gave rise to us, Neanderthals and Denisovans — the fork before the final three branches.
taxa.physicalChange: Brains as large as modern ones in a still-robust, heavy-browed skull and a powerful body.
taxa.behavioralChange: Hunted big game with wooden spears, used fire more routinely, and built shelters — increasingly complex foraging across two continents.

pressures.pressure: Planning-heavy hunting, fire use and social learning across variable Ice-Age climates.

taxa.certainty: Useful population-grade; the single species label is disputed.

image.creator: Cícero Moraes
image.caveat: One face cannot represent all Middle Pleistocene Homo; soft tissue is interpretive.

taxa.summary: Not primitive humans but a close sibling species — and many living people carry their DNA.
taxa.physicalChange: A stocky, powerful, cold-adapted body with a long low skull, big nose and a brain as large as ours.
taxa.behavioralChange: Skilled big-game hunters who made tools, used fire, cared for their injured, and buried some of their dead.

pressures.pressure: Surviving repeated Ice-Age cold in Europe and Asia.

taxa.certainty: Very well known from fossils and genomes; interbred with Homo sapiens.

image.creator: Henryk Fudali (sculpture)
image.caveat: Body proportions are fossil-based; hair, skin and clothing are interpretive.

taxa.summary: A whole human population first recognised from DNA, not bones — sister group to the Neanderthals.
taxa.physicalChange: Anatomy is still fragmentary (a finger bone, a jaw, some teeth), so much of their appearance is inferred from genes.
taxa.behavioralChange: Lived across Asia, including the high Tibetan Plateau, and passed high-altitude gene variants to living Tibetan people.

pressures.pressure: Living at low-oxygen Tibetan elevations.

taxa.certainty: Genomically certain; many fossil attributions are tentative.

image.creator: Wikimedia Commons
image.caveat: Denisovans are known mainly from DNA and fragments; no openly-licensed life reconstruction exists, so the defining Xiahe jaw is shown.

fossilSites.note: Finger bone that yielded the defining genome.

taxa.summary: Our own species — assembled in Africa from connected populations, then spread across the planet.
taxa.physicalChange: A high, rounded braincase, a flat face tucked under the brain, a chin, and a lightly built skeleton.
taxa.behavioralChange: Symbolic art, language, long-distance trade and ever-accumulating technology — culture became the main way we adapt.

pressures.pressure: Heavy reliance on social learning and culture rather than brute strength.

taxa.certainty: Our species' reality is certain; no single fossil is 'the first human'.

image.creator: Wikimedia Commons
image.caveat: A photograph of a living person representing our species today.

fossilSites.note: Among the earliest Homo sapiens fossils.
